full_text:
Functional HR testing

Three serial FFPE sections of each tumor specimen were deparaffinized in xylene and rehydrated in decreasing concentrations of ethanol. Sections were boiled in 10 mM citrate buffer, pH 6, for antigen retrieval, and immunostained according to standard procedures with primary antibodies against cytokeratin (panCK, C2931, Sigma Aldrich), γH2Ax (CST9718S, Cell Signaling Technology®), geminin (ab104306, Abcam), and RAD51 (ab133534, Abcam) in three combinations: panCK & γH2Ax, geminin & γH2Ax, and geminin & RAD51. Sections were then incubated with fluorescently labeled secondary antibodies (donkey anti-mouse IgG-Alexa-Fluor 488TM, A-21202, InvitrogenTM; and donkey anti-rabbit IgG-Alexa-Fluor 647TM, A-31573, InvitrogenTM) and nuclei were counterstained with Hoechst 33342 (InvitrogenTM). Slides were digitized using Pannoramic 250 FLASH III slide scanner (3DHISTECH) at 20x magnification.

Functional HR (fHR) scoring was performed as described previously (Pikkusaari et al. 2023). Briefly, regions of interest (ROIs) from each of the three staining combinations were analyzed in FIJI/ImageJ using custom macros. ROIs with high tumor cell content, marked by panCK, and with visible DNA damage, marked by γH2Ax, were chosen for analysis based on the panCK-γH2Ax–stained serial section. From the geminin-γH2Ax–stained section, the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei (geminin positive) with DNA damage (γH2Ax positive) was quantified. The fHR score was obtained from geminin-RAD51–stained sections as the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei undergoing HR-mediated repair, i.e., RAD51-positive nuclei, out of all S-G2 phase nuclei. As per previously established cut-off values, chemo-naïve samples with fHR score of <10 were classified as functionally HR-deficient, whereas samples with fHR score of >10 were classified as functionally HR-proficient. For chemo-treated samples, a cut-off of <30 was used for HR deficiency.

To the acknowledgements section:

Images were generated using 3DHISTECH Pannoramic 250 FLASH III digital slide scanner at Finnish Genome Editing Center supported by HiLIFE and the Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki, and Biocenter Finland.
